sections.heading: Step 1: Retrieve your Service Account access token for the project.
sections.body:
Use your service account to generate an access token, which
authenticates your requests to the Atlas Administration API.
To learn how to generate an access token, see
[Make an API (Application Programming Interface) Request](example-api-request).

sections.heading: Step 2: Make an  call.
sections.body:
Replace the following values in the `curl` command below. This
command sends a `POST` request to your cluster to
create a MongoDB Search index with the dateFacet field type.

- Replace \{`ACCESS-TOKEN`} with the output from the preceding step

- Replace \{`groupId`} with the project ID of the project where
  you want to create the MongoDB Search index

- Replace \{`clusterName`} with the name of the cluster where
  you want to create the MongoDB Search index

```shell
curl --header "Authorization: Bearer ${ACCESS_TOKEN}" \
    --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2025-03-12+json" \
    --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -X POST "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups/{groupId}/clusters/{clusterName}/search/indexes" \
    -d '{ "collectionName": "<collection>",
        "database": "<database>",
        "name": "default",
        "type": "search",
        "definition": {
            "mappings": { 
              "dynamic": true|false,
              "fields": { 
                "<field-name>": { 
                  "type": "uuid"
                } 
              }
            }
        }
    }'

```

The MongoDB Search dateFacet type takes the following parameters:

| Option | Type | Necessity | Description                                                               |
| ------ | ---- | --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| type   | uuid | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be uuid. |

Use [Universally Unique Identifiers](manual/reference/method/UUID) (UUIDs) when you need values that are virtually guaranteed to be unique.
You can use the MongoDB Search `uuid` type to index, query, and [sorting](sort-ref) over
[BSON Binary Subtype 4](manual/reference/bson-types) fields.

sections.heading: Step 1: Create a  configuration file and specify the  type.
Step 2: Connect to the Atlas CLI
sections.body:
In your terminal, connect to your cluster from the
Atlas CLI. To learn more, see
[Connect from the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli).

sections.heading: Step 3: Create a  index.
sections.body:
Run the [atlas clusters search indexes create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-clusters-search-indexes-create)
command in your terminal, replacing `<clusterName>` with the
name of the cluster, and `<path-to-file>` with the
path to the `search-index.json` file:

```shell
atlas clusters search indexes create --clusterName <cluster-name> --file <path-to-file>

```

The MongoDB Search dateFacet type takes the following parameters:

| Option | Type | Necessity | Description                                                               |
| ------ | ---- | --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| type   | uuid | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be uuid. |

Use [Universally Unique Identifiers](manual/reference/method/UUID) (UUIDs) when you need values that are virtually guaranteed to be unique.
You can use the MongoDB Search `uuid` type to index, query, and [sorting](sort-ref) over
[BSON Binary Subtype 4](manual/reference/bson-types) fields.

sections.heading: Step 1: Connect to your cluster using .
sections.body:
Open [Compass](https://www.mongodb.com/try/download/compass) and connect to your cluster. For
detailed instructions, see [Compass](atlas-connect-via-compass).

sections.heading: Step 2: Specify the database and collection.
sections.body: On the Database screen, click the name of the database, then click the name of the collection.

sections.heading: Step 3: Create the  index.
sections.body:
- Click the Indexes tab, then select Search Indexes.

- Click Create Atlas Search Index to open the index creation dialog box.

- Specify a name for the index.

  Your MongoDB Search index is named `default` by default. If you keep this name, then your index will be the
  default Search index for any MongoDB Search query that does not specify a different `index` option in
  its [operators](fts-operators). If you are creating multiple indexes, we recommend
  that you maintain a consistent, descriptive naming convention across your indexes.

- Specify the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) MongoDB Search index definition.

  ```json
  {
    "mappings": {
      "dynamic": true|false,
      "fields": {
        "<field-name>": {
          "type": "uuid"
        }
      }
    }
  }

  ```

- Click Create Search Index.

The MongoDB Search dateFacet type takes the following parameters:

| Option | Type | Necessity | Description                                                               |
| ------ | ---- | --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| type   | uuid | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be uuid. |

Use [Universally Unique Identifiers](manual/reference/method/UUID) (UUIDs) when you need values that are virtually guaranteed to be unique.
You can use the MongoDB Search `uuid` type to index, query, and [sorting](sort-ref) over
[BSON Binary Subtype 4](manual/reference/bson-types) fields.

sections.heading: Step 1: Connect to the deployment by using .
sections.body:
In your terminal, connect to your Atlas cloud-hosted
deployment or local deployment from [mongosh](mongodb-shell). For detailed
instructions on how to connect, see
[Connect to a Deployment](mongodb-shell/connect).

sections.heading: Step 2: Switch to the database that contains the collection for which you want to create the index.
sections.body:
**Input:**

```shell
 use <database>
```

**Output:**

```shell
switched to db <database>
```

sections.heading: Step 3: Run the  method to create the index.
sections.body:
**Input:**

```shell
db.<collection>.createSearchIndex(
  "default",
  {
    "mappings": {
      "dynamic": true|false,
      "fields": {
        "<field-name>": {
          "type": "uuid"
        }
      }
    }
  }
)
```

**Output:**

```
default
```

The MongoDB Search dateFacet type takes the following parameters:

| Option | Type | Necessity | Description                                                               |
| ------ | ---- | --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| type   | uuid | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be uuid. |

Use [Universally Unique Identifiers](manual/reference/method/UUID) (UUIDs) when you need values that are virtually guaranteed to be unique.
You can use the MongoDB Search `uuid` type to index, query, and [sorting](sort-ref) over
[BSON Binary Subtype 4](manual/reference/bson-types) fields.

Choose your preferred configuration method in the Atlas UI and then select the database and collection.

Visual Editor

- Click Refine Your Index to configure your index.

- In the Field Mappings section, click Add
  Field Mapping to open the Add Field Mapping window.

- Click Customized Configuration.

- Select the field to index from the Field Name dropdown.

  > **Note:** You can't index fields that contain the dollar (`$`) sign at the
  > start of the field name.

- Click the Data Type dropdown and select
  UUID.

- Configure the field properties for the dateFacet type. To
  learn more, see Field Properties.

- Click Add.

JSON Editor

The following is the JSON (Javascript Object Notation) syntax for the dateFacet type.
Replace the default index definition with the following. To learn more
about the fields, see Field Properties.

```json
{
  "mappings": {
    "dynamic": true|false,
    "fields": {
      "<field-name>": {
        "type": "uuid"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

The MongoDB Search dateFacet type takes the following parameters:

| Option | Type | Necessity | Description                                                               |
| ------ | ---- | --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| type   | uuid | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be uuid. |

Use [Universally Unique Identifiers](manual/reference/method/UUID) (UUIDs) when you need values that are virtually guaranteed to be unique.
You can use the MongoDB Search `uuid` type to index, query, and [sorting](sort-ref) over
[BSON Binary Subtype 4](manual/reference/bson-types) fields.

sections.heading: Step 1: Set up the C project.
sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` for this project:

```shell
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
```

Add the C driver to your project by following the instructions in the
[MongoDB C Driver documentation](languages/c/c-driver/current/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Set up a CMake application
sections.body:
To configure your application, create a `CMakeLists.txt` file in
your project directory. Then, add the following code to the file:

```txt
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (index.out create_index.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(index.out mongoc::mongoc)

```

The preceding code performs the following actions:

- Configures a C project.

- Creates a `index.out` executable for your application.

- Finds and requires the C driver. Replace the `<version>`
  placeholder with your C driver version, such as `2.0.0`.

- Links the program to the `libmongoc` library.

> **Note:** In the sample `CMakeLists.txt` file, the `mongoc::mongoc` target
> points to either the static library or the shared library.
> The library type depends on which one is available and
> whichever type the user specifies in the `MONGOC_DEFAULT_IMPORTED_LIBRARY_TYPE`
> CMake configuration setting. If you don't set this value and
> both library types are available, `mongoc::mongoc` uses
> the static library.
>
> You can use the `mongoc::static` target to explicitly use the
> static library or the `mongoc::shared` target to use the shared
> library.

codeBlocks.content:
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (index.out create_index.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(index.out mongoc::mongoc)

sections.heading: Step 4: Create the index.
sections.body:
In your terminal, run the following commands to build and run this
application:

**Input:**

```shell
cmake -S. -Bcmake-build
cmake --build cmake-build --target index.out
./cmake-build/index.out
```

**Output:**

```
Index created!
```

The MongoDB Search dateFacet type takes the following parameters:

| Option | Type | Necessity | Description                                                               |
| ------ | ---- | --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| type   | uuid | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be uuid. |

Use [Universally Unique Identifiers](manual/reference/method/UUID) (UUIDs) when you need values that are virtually guaranteed to be unique.
You can use the MongoDB Search `uuid` type to index, query, and [sorting](sort-ref) over
[BSON Binary Subtype 4](manual/reference/bson-types) fields.

sections.heading: Step 1: Set up and initialize the .NET/C# project.
sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` and initialize your project in that directory:

```shell
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
dotnet new console

# Add the MongoDB .NET/C# Driver to your project
dotnet add package MongoDB.Driver
```

For more detailed installation instructions, see the
[MongoDB C# Driver documentation](csharp/get-started).

codeBlocks.content:
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
dotnet new console

# Add the MongoDB .NET/C# Driver to your project
dotnet add package MongoDB.Driver

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Create the index.
sections.body:
**Input:**

```shell
c++ --std=c++17 CreateIndex.cpp $(pkg-config --cflags --libs libmongocxx) -o ./app.out
./app.out
```

**Output:**

```
New index name: default
```

> **Tip:** 
>
> MacOS Error
>
> MacOS users might see the following error after running the preceding
> commands:
>
> ```sh
> dyld[54430]: Library not loaded: @rpath/libmongocxx._noabi.dylib
> ```
>
> To resolve this error, use the `-Wl`,\`\`-rpath\`\` linker option to set
> the `@rpath`, as shown in the following code:
>
> ```sh
> c++ --std=c++17 CreateIndex.cpp -Wl,-rpath,/usr/local/lib/ $(pkg-config --cflags --libs libmongocxx) -o ./app.out
> ./app.out
> ```

The MongoDB Search dateFacet type takes the following parameters:

| Option | Type | Necessity | Description                                                               |
| ------ | ---- | --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| type   | uuid | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be uuid. |

You can use the MongoDB Search `uuid` type to index, query, and [sorting](sort-ref) over
[BSON Binary Subtype 4](manual/reference/bson-types) fields.

sections.heading: Step 1: Set up your Java project with the MongoDB Java driver.
sections.body:
In your IDE, create a new [Maven](https://maven.apache.org/) or
[Gradle](https://gradle.org/) project. Add the Bill of
Materials (BOM) for MongoDB JVM artifacts to your project to
organize dependency versions. The BOM simplifies dependency
management by ensuring that you maintain consistent and compatible
versions of dependencies, such as between the Java driver and
the core driver library. Use the BOM to avoid version conflicts
and simplify upgrades.

Select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs
to view instructions for adding the BOM for each dependency manager:

Maven

Add the following code to the `dependencyManagement` list in your
`pom.xml` file:

```xml
<dependencyManagement>
    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>
            <artifactId>mongodb-driver-bom</artifactId>
            <version>5.5.1</version>
            <type>pom</type>
            <scope>import</scope>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>
```

Gradle

Add the following code to dependencies list in your
`build.gradle` file:

```groovy
dependencies {
    implementation(platform("org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-bom:5.5.1"))
}
```

To view a list of dependencies that the BOM manages, see
the [mongodb-driver-bom dependency listing](https://mvnrepository.com/artifact/org.mongodb/mongodb-driver-bom/5.51)
on the Maven Repository website.

After adding the BOM, select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs to view
instructions for adding the MongoDB Java Sync Driver as a dependency
in your project:

Maven

Add the following code to the `dependencies` array in your Maven
project's `pom.xml` file:

```xml
<dependencies>
    <dependency>    
      <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>    
      <artifactId>mongodb-driver-sync</artifactId>    
      <version>4.11.1</version>    
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

```

Gradle

Add the following to the `dependencies` array in your Gradle
project's `build.gradle` file:

```json
dependencies {
   // MongoDB Java Sync Driver v4.11.0 or later
   implementation 'org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-sync'
}

```

Run your package manager to install the dependencies to your project.

For more detailed installation instructions and version compatibility, see
the [MongoDB Java Driver documentation](java/sync/current/get-started).

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
sections.body:
Create a `create_index.py` file in your project directory,
and copy and paste the following code into the file.

```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# Connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

database = client["<database>"]
collection = database["<collection>"]

# Create the MongoDB Search index definition for the UUID field
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": true|false,
            "fields": {
                "<field-name>":{
                   "type": "uuid"
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# Create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")

```

Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.

Atlas Cluster

Your connection string should use the following format:

```
mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
```

To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

Local or Self-Managed

Your connection string should use the
following format:

```
mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
```

To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).

sections.heading: Step 3: Create the index.

# Create a new directory for the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Set up a CMake application
sections.body:
To configure your application, create a `CMakeLists.txt` file in
your project directory. Then, add the following code to the file:

```txt
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (index.out create_index.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(index.out mongoc::mongoc)

```

The preceding code performs the following actions:

- Configures a C project.

- Creates a `index.out` executable for your application.

- Finds and requires the C driver. Replace the `<version>`
  placeholder with your C driver version, such as `2.0.0`.

- Links the program to the `libmongoc` library.

> **Note:** In the sample `CMakeLists.txt` file, the `mongoc::mongoc` target
> points to either the static library or the shared library.
> The library type depends on which one is available and
> whichever type the user specifies in the `MONGOC_DEFAULT_IMPORTED_LIBRARY_TYPE`
> CMake configuration setting. If you don't set this value and
> both library types are available, `mongoc::mongoc` uses
> the static library.
>
> You can use the `mongoc::static` target to explicitly use the
> static library or the `mongoc::shared` target to use the shared
> library.

codeBlocks.content:
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.11)
project(atlas-search-index LANGUAGES C)
add_executable (index.out create_index.c)
find_package(mongoc <version> REQUIRED)
target_link_libraries(index.out mongoc::mongoc)

sections.heading: Step 4: Create the index.
sections.body:
In your terminal, run the following commands to build and run this
application:

**Input:**

```shell
cmake -S. -Bcmake-build
cmake --build cmake-build --target index.out
./cmake-build/index.out
```

**Output:**

```
Index created!
```

The MongoDB Search dateFacet type takes the following parameters:

| Option | Type | Necessity | Description                                                               |
| ------ | ---- | --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| type   | uuid | Required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be uuid. |

Use [Universally Unique Identifiers](manual/reference/method/UUID) (UUIDs) when you need values that are virtually guaranteed to be unique.
You can use the MongoDB Search `uuid` type to index, query, and [sorting](sort-ref) over
[BSON Binary Subtype 4](manual/reference/bson-types) fields.

sections.heading: Step 1: Set up and initialize the .NET/C# project.
sections.body:
In your terminal, navigate to where you want to create your application,
then run the following command to create a directory called
`atlas-search-project` and initialize your project in that directory:

```shell
# Create a new directory and initialize the project
mkdir atlas-search-project && cd atlas-search-project
dotnet new console

# Add the MongoDB .NET/C# Driver to your project
dotnet add package MongoDB.Driver
```

For more detailed installation instructions, see the
[MongoDB C# Driver documentation](csharp/get-started).

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
Step 3: Create the index.
sections.body:
**Input:**

```shell
c++ --std=c++17 CreateIndex.cpp $(pkg-config --cflags --libs libmongocxx) -o ./app.out
./app.out
```

**Output:**

```
New index name: default
```

> **Tip:** 
>
> MacOS Error
>
> MacOS users might see the following error after running the preceding
> commands:
>
> ```sh
> dyld[54430]: Library not loaded: @rpath/libmongocxx._noabi.dylib
> ```
>
> To resolve this error, use the `-Wl`,\`\`-rpath\`\` linker option to set
> the `@rpath`, as shown in the following code:
>
> ```sh
> c++ --std=c++17 CreateIndex.cpp -Wl,-rpath,/usr/local/lib/ $(pkg-config --cflags --libs libmongocxx) -o ./app.out
> ./app.out
> ```

| Option | Type | Necessity | Description                                                               |
| ------ | ---- | --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |

You can use the MongoDB Search `uuid` type to index, query, and [sorting](sort-ref) over
[BSON Binary Subtype 4](manual/reference/bson-types) fields.

sections.heading: Step 1: Set up your Java project with the MongoDB Java driver.
sections.body:
In your IDE, create a new [Maven](https://maven.apache.org/) or
[Gradle](https://gradle.org/) project. Add the Bill of
Materials (BOM) for MongoDB JVM artifacts to your project to
organize dependency versions. The BOM simplifies dependency
management by ensuring that you maintain consistent and compatible
versions of dependencies, such as between the Java driver and
the core driver library. Use the BOM to avoid version conflicts
and simplify upgrades.

Select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs
to view instructions for adding the BOM for each dependency manager:

Maven

Add the following code to the `dependencyManagement` list in your
`pom.xml` file:

```xml
<dependencyManagement>
    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>
            <artifactId>mongodb-driver-bom</artifactId>
            <version>5.5.1</version>
            <type>pom</type>
            <scope>import</scope>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>
```

Gradle

Add the following code to dependencies list in your
`build.gradle` file:

```groovy
dependencies {
    implementation(platform("org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-bom:5.5.1"))
}
```

To view a list of dependencies that the BOM manages, see
the [mongodb-driver-bom dependency listing](https://mvnrepository.com/artifact/org.mongodb/mongodb-driver-bom/5.51)
on the Maven Repository website.

After adding the BOM, select from the following **Maven** and **Gradle** tabs to view
instructions for adding the MongoDB Java Sync Driver as a dependency
in your project:

Maven

Add the following code to the `dependencies` array in your Maven
project's `pom.xml` file:

```xml
<dependencies>
    <dependency>    
      <groupId>org.mongodb</groupId>    
      <artifactId>mongodb-driver-sync</artifactId>    
      <version>4.11.1</version>    
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

```

Gradle

Add the following to the `dependencies` array in your Gradle
project's `build.gradle` file:

```json
dependencies {
   // MongoDB Java Sync Driver v4.11.0 or later
   implementation 'org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-sync'
}

```

Run your package manager to install the dependencies to your project.

For more detailed installation instructions and version compatibility, see
the [MongoDB Java Driver documentation](java/sync/current/get-started).

# Create a new directory for the project

# Install PyMongo

sections.heading: Step 2: Define the index.
sections.body:
Create a `create_index.py` file in your project directory,
and copy and paste the following code into the file.

```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# Connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

database = client["<database>"]
collection = database["<collection>"]

# Create the MongoDB Search index definition for the UUID field
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": true|false,
            "fields": {
                "<field-name>":{
                   "type": "uuid"
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# Create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")

```

Replace `<connection-string>` with the connection string for your
Atlas cluster or local Atlas deployment.

Atlas Cluster

Your connection string should use the following format:

```
mongodb+srv://<db_username>:<db_password>@<clusterName>.<hostname>.mongodb.net
```

To learn more, see [Driver](connect-via-driver).

Local or Self-Managed

Your connection string should use the
following format:

```
mongodb://localhost:<port-number>/?directConnection=true
```

To learn more, see [Connection Strings](manual/reference/connection-string).
